Document:
# Rule 3 - You must play fairly Space Station 14 is a game that relies on the collaboration of the players. Players must adhere to self-imposed restrictions that aren't possible to implement mechanically. Monkestation does not allow [bold]powergaming[/bold], [bold]metagaming[/bold],[bold]metagrudging[/bold], [bold]self-antagging[/bold] or [bold]validhunting[/bold] and requires some form of [bold]escalation[/bold] before engaging in mechanics. Moreover, using external means to give yourself an advantage over other players such as "Metacommunications" and exploits is prohibited.
##Definitions ###Powergaming The prioritization of amassing mechanical advantages for yourself, to the detriment of the enjoyment of others. A non-exhaustive list of examples are: - Buying guns and armor as a cargo technician to hunt traitors - Rushing roundstart to make a stun prod and makeshift cuffs without a good reason - Growing instantly lethal death-nettles as a botanist
Most importantly, powergaming is dependent on the current situation. All of these examples might be acceptable if the station is being attacked by an elite team of nuclear operatives, but not against a single traitor with a mosin.
###Metagaming Using Out-Of-Character (OOC) knowledge in an In-Character (IC) manner. Here is a non-exhaustive list of examples: - Giving extended access to your Out-Of-Character (OOC) friend without a proper In-Character (IC) reason. - Using your time as a ghost between revivals to locate an item or person. - Checking a recently caught traitor for a storage implant without any indication that they had one.
###Metagrudging Unjustified or unprovoked griefing towards a player who has wronged you in a previous round. While actions that occur in previous rounds can be remembered in-character, they shouldn't drastically affect your actions towards them in future rounds. Here is a non-exhaustive list of examples: - Arresting an assistant because they were a traitor last round, even if you have no reason to this round. - Being openly hostile towards a specific character because a few rounds ago they blew up your department. - Killing a specific person every time you roll traitor because you ''just don't like them.''
Metagrudging is distinct from having prior knowledge of a static character's personality and behavior. If your character's personality is well known to the community, then you should expect people to know how your character thinks and acts.
###Self-Antagging Acting against the station, the station's goals or its crew without mechanically being an antagonist. Here is a non-exhaustive list of examples: - Killing an assistant for taking all of the food from the kitchen. - Stealing all of the materials from the ore silo and selling them. - Hacking into the HoS's office and stealing their hardsuit.
Being a criminal within your character's backstory is one thing, but you are still employed by Nanotrasen and should act like it. Committing small crimes or building dangerous machines can breathe life into mundane rounds, but should not cross the line of murder or mass destruction. A good rule of thumb to know whether you are currently self-antagging or not is to put yourself in other player's shoes and ask yourself if this situation would be fun if you were living it. This unfortunately cannot be applied to all situations, so use your best judgement. \nRemember you are sharing the server with other human beings.
###Valid Hunting Hunting antagonists as a non-security job or as a job that is not allowed. Here is a non-exhaustive list of examples: - A janitor chasing a traitor through maintenance because they placed a bug in the custodial closet. - A medical doctor picking up the stun baton from a murdered security officer and chasing the responsible criminal into the halls. - A miner using their lavaland loot to destroy a bloodsucker's lair.
Some antagonists are validhuntable, like nuclear operatives, wizards, or xenoborgs. Some others, like traitors, thieves and ninjas should be left to the highly capable hands of the station's security force. \nTypically, station-ending antagonists are validhuntable, meanwhile smaller threats are not, as they can be dealt with by your security department alone. \nLike powergaming, valid hunting is context dependent. If a traitor has killed 70% of the station and security is in shambles, it is reasonable for a janitor to take up arms to help defend the station. \nNon-security crew are allowed to defend their department within reason, but should not actively pursue antagonists.
###Escalation Signalling to other players that you intend to either aggravate a situation, or end roleplay and begin using mechanics. \nEscalation is context dependent, but it can be as simple as hailing a suspect walking down the hall, or be more intense, like emoting "pulls out their sword and takes a fighting stance!" for more aggressive situations. \n'On-Sight' escalation (attacking a player as soon as you see them, without RP) interactions should only be used for visible validhuntable antags, or antags that have escalated to that degree (i.e. bombings, threats over the radio). \nLike powergaming and valid hunting, escalation is context dependent. The more intense the intended mechanics are, the more intense the escalation.
###Metacommunications Using methods to communicate with others outside of the game. - Talking to friends about the ongoing round. - Sharing screenshots of the ongoing round on discord. - Coordinating with other players using voice chat.
